name: Shipping / Tanker Rates
summary: Day 40. War risk insurance UNCHANGED at $10-14M/voyage (~30,000x pre-crisis) despite ceasefire announcement. Lloyd's: physical normalisation 'sequential, multi-party process not compressed by political will, executive order, or diplomatic breakthrough.' Brent -13.5% prices faster restoration than operationally possible. ~40 VLCCs anchored 5-day waits at Yanbu. Frontline 1Y charters $76,900/day; spot AG-Far East ~$112K/day TCE down from $445K mid-March peak (traffic stopped, not risk reduced). Hormuz tanker traffic still 80%+ collapsed. Reinsurance restoration takes weeks at minimum.

signals.content: VLCC rates hit all-time high of $423,736/day on Baltic Exchange (TD3C MEG-China), up 94% in single day — highest in nearly 50-year history of the index. War risk insurance being cancelled by major providers.
VLCC Kalamos set individual vessel record at $770,000/day, loading at Yanbu for India's west coast. Normal rates: $80,000-100,000/day. Current rates imply freight cost adds $7/bbl to oil.
Cape of Good Hope crossings increased 89%. Ships rerouting around Africa adding 10-14 days to transit. CMA CGM introduced $3,000/FEU emergency surcharge.
150 ships stranded around Strait of Hormuz. Insurance companies cancelling war risk coverage for vessels in Gulf.

Marine war risk insurance premiums up 1,000%+ — hull war risk premium now ~$7.5 million per trip (up from ~$625,000 pre-conflict). Rate at 3% of hull value, up from 0.2%. Lloyd's List reports Gulf premiums 'topping double-digit millions per trip.' Many insurers stopped covering Hormuz transit entirely, making strait 'de facto closed' for commercial vessels without government backstop. US government created $20 billion maritime reinsurance facility via DFC with Chubb as lead underwriter to keep shipping moving. Container freight: Drewry World Container Index at $2,172/40ft (Mar 19), up third consecutive week. CMA CGM raised surcharge to $265/TEU. Iran operating selective blockade — only 16 AIS-visible crossings in most recent 7-day window. Frontline (FRO) +2.1% to $34.55 as tanker demand remains extreme.

VLCC rates at 50-year all-time highs: $401K-$445K/day on Baltic Exchange (TD3C), with some fixtures at $538K-$770K/day. War risk insurance at 1-7.5% of hull value ($2-15M extra per VLCC transit), up from 0.25% pre-war. Some insurers suspended Gulf coverage entirely. Market bifurcated: Western operators (Frontline +0.2% despite record rates) locked out by insurance costs, while IRGC-tolled yuan passages serve Chinese/non-Western vessels at $2M/transit. Container freight: ~$2,200/FEU global, $4,000+ Shanghai-Dubai (+110%). Emergency surcharges on Gulf routes.

Kuwaiti tanker Al-Salmi attacked by Iranian drone at Dubai Port overnight (March 31, ~12:10 AM). Fire extinguished by 4:30 AM. All 24 crew safe. No oil leakage. One of the most significant maritime attacks of the war — attacking a neutral country's vessel in UAE territorial waters. Undermines 'selective Hormuz opening' narrative: Iran simultaneously allowing some ships through while attacking others at port. Insurance implications severe — if vessels not safe at port, war risk premiums for entire Gulf reprice higher. Iran has NOT claimed or denied. Also: Houthi still zero confirmed Red Sea attacks (Day 32) — deliberately preserving shipping card as leverage. Houthis told Lloyd's List 'no reason to prevent' Yanbu VLCC trade 'at present' — revealing deliberate restraint. Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC rerouted or suspended Red Sea/Suez. Bab al-Mandeb at ~57 transits/24hr vs normal. CONFIRMED (multiple independent sources).

Frontline +4.4% DESPITE oil pullback — the structural tanker story. VLCC day rates at historic records: $424K-$758K/day (Clarksons, Splash247). Adamantios at world-record $537,913/day to Reliance. Kalamos at $758K/day to BPCL. War risk insurance premiums 0.25% → up to 3% of vessel value ($6M+ per Hormuz transit). Ships rerouting around Africa → longer voyage = fewer available vessels = rate surge. Tanker rates driven by voyage DISTANCE and vessel AVAILABILITY, not oil price direction. Even with oil -6.6%, rerouting persists regardless of peace rhetoric. Insurance premiums take months to normalize. Most INELASTIC crisis beneficiary. Day 33 zero Houthi Red Sea shipping attacks — commercial closure via carrier withdrawal (Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC rerouted), not military blockade. Al-Salmi VLCC attacked at Dubai port anchorage — insurance must reprice for entire Gulf, not just Hormuz. (Sources: Clarksons, Splash247, Maritime Executive, Bloomberg, UKMTO)

CONFIRMED: Iran struck QatarEnergy-chartered tanker AQUA 1 near Ras Laffan (17nm north) with 3 cruise missiles — 2 intercepted, 1 hit. 21 crew safe. IRGC claimed responsibility, alleged Israeli ties. Iran reportedly gave Qatar advance notice — calculated escalation, not indiscriminate. Second vessel attack in 2 days (after Al-Salmi VLCC at Dubai port). Attack pattern expanding from Hormuz chokepoint to Gulf-wide — Dubai port, then Qatar waters. (Sources: QatarEnergy, Al Jazeera, AGBI)

CONFIRMED: VLCC rates remain in 'never ever before' territory. Peak $601,569/day; now $112-117K/day — still 3-5x pre-crisis levels. Lloyd's List notes rates partly 'imaginary' — illiquid market, panic fixing. Key structural signal: Frontline tanker stock -0.1% FLAT despite Brent crude -10.5% — shipping disruption is more entrenched than the financial oil premium. Tanker rates driven by voyage distance and vessel availability, not oil price. (Sources: Lloyd's List, Baltic Exchange, yfinance)

CONFIRMED: Container freight (Drewry WCI) at $2,279/FEU, +5% WoW — 4th consecutive weekly increase. Shanghai-Jebel Ali spiked from $1,800 to $4,000+. Surcharges $800-$4,000/container across Gulf routes. Container shipping disruption widening beyond tanker market. (Sources: Drewry)

Frontline tankers +5.1% to $36.60 — shipping stocks rallying on war continuation signal. VLCC rates at $112-117K/day (down from $601K peak but still 3-5x pre-crisis). AQUA 1 tanker (QatarEnergy-chartered) struck near Ras Laffan April 1. Al-Salmi VLCC hit at Dubai anchorage March 31. Two shipping attacks in 2 days — both in Gulf ally waters, not Hormuz. War risk insurance: 1%+ hull value ($10-14M/voyage). Container freight WCI: $2,279/FEU (+5% WoW). Maersk/CMA CGM/Hapag-Lloyd rerouting to Cape. (Sources: yfinance, Lloyd's List, Baltic Exchange, Al Jazeera)

Frontline +5.1% to $36.60. VLCC rates $45,941-55,010/day (ME-China) — down from $445K peak but still 3-5x pre-crisis. War risk insurance 1%+ hull value ($10-14M/voyage), 5x pre-crisis. Lloyd's JWC expanded to Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, Oman. Container freight rising: Asia-Med +15% WoW to $3,850/FEU. BOAT ETF +1.4%. (Sources: Baltic Exchange, Lloyd's List, Freightos, yfinance)

NEW: US DFC + Chubb announced $40B marine reinsurance facility ($20B DFC-backed, $20B from Chubb/AIG/Berkshire/CNA/Liberty/Starr/Travelers). US government backstopping Gulf shipping insurance. This reduces 'invisible siege' mechanism — if commercial insurance won't cover transits, government will. Net effect: some tankers may resume Gulf transit, marginally easing supply constraint. BUT physical risk remains — insurance doesn't stop missiles. Separately, Hezbollah CLAIMS (unverified) cruise missile hit on Israeli warship 68nm off Lebanese coast — if confirmed, represents naval threat escalation for all shipping. Container freight rising: Asia-US West $2,127/FEU (+8% WoW), Asia-Med $3,850/FEU (+15% WoW), Asia-US East $3,069/FEU. 20,000 seafarers stranded. Day 38 zero Houthi Red Sea attacks.

Hormuz weekly transits hit war-high: 21 ships over April 5-6 weekend (Bloomberg Tracker). Yanbu congestion worsening: 32 supertankers stacked (up from 27). March handled 47 VLCC loadings — 4x pre-war monthly average of 11-12. Port throughput ~4M bpd vs 7M bpd pipeline delivery — port is binding constraint. Saudi informed customers April allocations reduced. VLCC day rates moderated from March peak $445K/day to ~$55K/day MEG-China — chaos premium absorbed, stabilizing at elevated level. Frontline -0.3% despite oil +3.1% — structural repricing embedded (+59% 3M), daily moves now stable. Marine war risk insurance still 1.5-3.0% of hull value (US/UK/Israeli ships at 3%), $10-14M per VLCC trip. Container freight: Asia-Med +15%, Asia-USWC +8%. (Sources: Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker, House of Saud, Fortune, Seatrade Maritime, Freightos, Lloyd's List)

VLCC ME-China rates $200K-400K/day spot range (still 3-10x pre-crisis); peak $424K hit March 3. Down from peak; still extreme. Hormuz transits at WAR HIGHS per Bloomberg Tracker — but selectively under Iran's three-tier system, NOT a free reopening. War risk insurance $10-14M/voyage; 1%+ hull value; Hapag-Lloyd $3,500/container surcharge. Lloyd's: 'Safety not insurance' is what's stopping ships. 88% of Lloyd's market still underwriting at extreme premiums. The bifurcation: Iran-friendly flags transit; Western/Israeli flags do not. Frontline (FRO) $36.48 / -0.3% 1D / +7.8% 1M / +59.0% 3M — mild giveback off $36.60.

EVENING UPDATE: VLCC rates moderated from $424K/day peak to ~$112K/day (WS126.5) — NOT because risk decreased but because almost no one is transiting the Gulf. Lloyd's List: ME VLCC rates 'imaginary' due to so few fixtures. Frontline -0.3% — paradox: tanker stocks should rally on disruption but revenue depends on voyage count. Container freight $2,287/FEU (flat w/w, +29-31% since Feb). War risk surcharges $1,500-4,000/container. Bunker costs doubled. US government doubled Hormuz reinsurance from $20B to $40B DFC (April 3). (Sources: Baltic Exchange, Lloyd's List, Drewry, S&P Global)

WAR RISK INSURANCE UNCHANGED ON CEASEFIRE — LLOYD'S: 'PHYSICAL NORMALISATION NOT COMPRESSED BY POLITICAL WILL' [CONFIRMED]. Despite Brent crashing -13.5% on the ceasefire announcement, Lloyd's List notes that hull war cover for Hormuz transit remains at $10-14M per voyage (1% of hull value, ~30,000x pre-crisis pricing). Lloyd's: 'Even if a ceasefire takes hold, reinsurers must rebuild risk models from scratch. Each vessel seeking Gulf transit must be individually re-underwritten on a voyage-by-voyage basis. Pricing must clear across the entire reinsurance chain, from treaty reinsurer to P&I club to shipowner. Capacity that was withdrawn at the treaty level must be reinstated through negotiation, not announcement. This is a sequential, multi-party process that cannot be compressed by political will, executive order, or diplomatic breakthrough.' Implication: physical Hormuz flow restoration takes WEEKS even on a holding ceasefire. The Brent -13.5% move prices a faster restoration than is operationally possible. ~40 VLCCs remain anchored 5-day waits at Yanbu. Frontline (FRO) 1Y charter rates at $76,900/day vs spot at AG-Far East ~$112K/day TCE (down from $445K mid-March peak — TRAFFIC stopped, not risk reduced).

edges.context: Hormuz closure + toll law = primary cause of rate disruption. AQUA 1 attack at Qatar waters + Al-Salmi at Dubai = Iran projecting force Gulf-wide. Lloyd's JWC redesignated entire Arabian Gulf as conflict zone. War risk insurance at 1%+ hull value. Tanker traffic collapsed 80%+.

Higher shipping rates add $7/bbl to delivered cost. Frontline FLAT vs Brent -10.5% — shipping disruption more structural than oil premium. VLCC rates 3-5x pre-crisis. Insurance costs making Gulf transit uneconomic.
India's imports face longer routes and much higher shipping costs on top of higher commodity prices.

Day 34 zero confirmed Houthi Red Sea shipping attacks. Bab al-Mandeb in commercial closure via carrier withdrawal. Bloomberg: Iran pressing Houthis to resume but 'divisions within leadership.' Houthis told Lloyd's List 'no reason to prevent' Yanbu trade 'at present' — preserving shipping card as leverage. Washington and Riyadh told European allies they believe Houthis 'currently seek to avoid further escalation.'

trigger_points.condition: Houthis begin confirmed Red Sea attacks (verified by maritime trackers)
trigger_points.mechanism: Double chokepoint: Hormuz AND Red Sea blocked. Only Cape of Good Hope remains. Shipping rates would spike further from already-historic levels. As of Day 33, Houthi threats remain rhetoric only — zero attacks confirmed.
